title: Circular Linked List
description: The last node points back to head instead of nullptr — no natural end, a different traversal stopping condition, and the round-robin use case.
objectives: Understand how a circular linked list differs structurally
Traverse a circular linked list without an nullptr to stop at
Build a simple circular linked list
Recognize round-robin scheduling as the classic use case

sections.text: 1. Recap: Linear Lists End at nullptr
Every list built so far — Singly Linked List, Doubly Linked List — had a clear end: the last node's next was nullptr. A circular linked list removes that ending entirely.
2. What is a Circular Linked List?
A circular linked list is a linked list where the last node's next pointer points back to the first node (head) instead of nullptr, forming a continuous loop with no natural end.

Recall Loops' infinite-loop warning, and Stack Memory's stack overflow from runaway recursion — this is the same fundamental danger, applied here: current never becomes nullptr on a circular list, since the last node loops back to head instead. The stopping condition must check something else entirely: whether we've returned to where we started.
4. Correct Traversal: Stop When You Return to head

Recall do-while from its own topic: it's the natural fit here, since the body must run at least once before the check ("have we returned to head?") makes sense — a while loop checking current != head before ever printing head itself would incorrectly print nothing for a single-node-just-visited list.

Circular linked lists naturally model anything that cycles endlessly with no true 'last turn' — like round-robin CPU scheduling, where each process gets a turn, and after the last process, the cycle simply continues back to the first one again, indefinitely.

sections.title: Musical chairs, or a scavenger hunt that loops back to the start
sections.text: Recall the scavenger hunt boxes. A circular linked list is that hunt with the last box's note pointing back to the very first box, instead of saying "the end" — the hunt simply keeps going in a loop forever, exactly like players taking turns around a table, cycling back to the first player after the last one goes.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Traversing a circular linked list with while (current != nullptr) — this becomes an infinite loop, since nullptr is never reached.
Using a plain while (current != head) without first entering the loop body — this skips printing head itself; do-while is usually the right structure here.
Forgetting to explicitly set the last node's next back to head when building the list — without that line, it's just an ordinary (non-circular) linked list.
Assuming circular linked lists always need special-case handling everywhere — many operations still work normally, as long as the traversal stopping condition accounts for the loop.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What makes a linked list circular?
questions.answer: The last node's next pointer points back to the head node instead of nullptr, forming a continuous loop with no natural end.

questions.question: Why can't you traverse a circular linked list with while (current != nullptr)?
questions.answer: Because nullptr is never reached — the last node loops back to head, so that condition never becomes false, causing an infinite loop.

questions.question: What is a classic real-world use case for a circular linked list?
questions.answer: Round-robin scheduling, where each item takes a turn in sequence and the cycle continues back to the first item after the last, with no true final turn.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A circular linked list differs from a regular one by making its last node's next point back to head instead of nullptr, forming a continuous loop with no natural end. This changes how traversal must be written: checking for nullptr causes an infinite loop, so the stopping condition instead checks whether the current pointer has returned to head — commonly implemented with a do-while loop, since the body needs to run once before that check makes sense. Circular linked lists naturally model anything that cycles indefinitely with no true final turn, like round-robin scheduling.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Circular Linked List
sections.text: Circular = the last box's note points back to the FIRST box, not 'the end.' Traverse with do-while, stop when you're back at head — nullptr never comes.
LeetCode: Circular Linked List in Practice

sections.title: Linked List Cycle
sections.problemStatement: Given the head of a linked list, return true if some node's next eventually points back to an earlier node, forming a cycle — exactly this lesson's circular structure, but possibly unintentional.

examples.input: head = [3,2,0,-4], tail connects to node index 1

hints: This lesson's do-while traversal deliberately stops when current returns to head — but what if a list becomes circular BY ACCIDENT (a bug), and you don't already know where 'head' of the loop is?
Floyd's slow/fast pointer technique detects any cycle without needing to know the loop's starting point in advance.
If fast ever equals slow again after they start moving, a cycle exists — if fast reaches nullptr first, the list has a genuine end and is not circular.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Track every visited node's address in a hash set — a repeat visit means a cycle.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Floyd's slow/fast pointers detect a cycle with O(1) extra space — no set of visited addresses needed.
